title: Te Whakatau Tohu o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku / Mapping the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will explore the mathematics of water flow, volume, and quality monitoring through the lens of kaitiakitanga for Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream that flows through our rohe and holds cultural importance for Ngāti Kahungunu as a traditional food gathering site and part of our whakapapa
learningObjectives: Calculate and compare the volume of water flowing through different sections of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku using appropriate units of measurement
Create scaled maps of the stream showing key features and mathematical measurements using coordinate geometry
Analyze water quality data using statistical measures (mean, median, mode) to identify patterns and trends
Apply mathematical reasoning to propose data-informed solutions for stream restoration and protection

activities.title: Te Awa Tātari - Stream Analysis
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku to measure stream width, depth, and flow rate at three different points, calculating volume using appropriate formulae and units

activities.title: Mahere Whakatau - Mapping with Coordinates
activities.description: Using GPS coordinates and traditional landmarks, students create scaled maps of the stream, plotting key features and calculating distances between points using coordinate geometry

activities.title: Tohu Wai Māori - Water Quality Statistics
activities.description: Students collect and analyze water quality data (pH, clarity, temperature) using statistical measures to identify patterns and assess the health of the awa

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Pāngarau - Mathematical Reasoning for Kaitiakitanga
activities.description: Working with local kaumātua, students use their mathematical findings to propose data-informed solutions for stream protection and present their reasoning

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua with knowledge of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
Hawke's Bay Regional Council stream monitoring toolkit and data
Topographic maps of the Mangakōtukutuku area from LINZ
Digital measuring tools (calipers, flow meters, water testing kits)

tasks: Submit a detailed mathematical report with calculations of stream volume and flow rates
Present scaled maps with coordinate geometry showing key stream features
Analyze water quality data using statistical measures and identify trends
Propose and justify data-informed solutions for stream protection

nzcAlignment: Geometry and Measurement: Use appropriate units and devices to measure length, area, volume, and capacity
Statistics: Compare and describe the variation between theoretical and experimental distributions in situations that involve elements of chance
Number and Algebra: Use a range of multiplicative strategies when operating on whole numbers and fractions
Te Tiriti o Waitangi: Applying mātauranga Māori and mathematical thinking to environmental kaitiakitanga
